body_txt: The Hero is a character who overcomes adversity and challenges by performing great acts that demonstrate their strength (physical or mental), ingenuity, and valor. In modern works, heroes are also often the primary source of goodness in the story or will fight on its behalf against a Big Bad . In contrast, heroes of classical works frequently engaged in heroics to protect their honor, pride, and vanity, as past societies put much more stock into such values. In this classical conception, being The Hero was more about accomplishing some great feat against a worthy opponent, rather than upholding and protecting some noble intention. While The Hero is often The Protagonist and The Leader , these tropes are not the same. The Protagonist is the principal character of the story who drives the plot forward. The Hero tends to be The Protagonist since baked into the hero's definition is a basic narrative structure for storytellers to follow or play with (hero faces some challenge and has to figure out a way to overcome it) and Hero Protagonist covers all such instances. That said, the hero is not always the protagonist of a story; see Villain Protagonist and Hero Antagonist for more on that. The Leader , on the other hand, is simply a character who sits at the head of some group or organization in-universe. It's a character trait that doesn't inherently have any bearing on the character's narrative role or plot function. Often times The Hero will also be The Leader of a group because idealized versions of both share many of the same traits: strong, resilient, righteous, noble, proactive, morally upstanding, well-liked, charismatic, confident, brave, etc. But once again, not every hero is The Leader and vice versa. See the list below for variations on this archetype and place all examples under one of these subtropes. For other tropes about or related to The Hero, see Hero Tropes .
